sentences.text: Yes.
I was born in Warsaw, Poland in 1920 to a middle-class family with old-fashioned values.
Education was one of the most important thing.
And my parents and me and my sister took private school to obtain a better education.
Good manners were important at that time, what counts.
So, of course, friends were approved or disapprove.
We were encouraged to read to try to develop our mental capacity for further learning.
The life was rather pleasant for us, the childhood, besides school.
They were nice.
At parks, nice.
Entertainment for the children.
And we tried [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to get everything that was there, I think.
Of course, there was a lot of love and also, what I realize now, a lot of protection against the reality of life, because I know it wasn't easy for my family economically and otherwise.
But, still, my sister and me were spared of all the hardship that the family went through as much as possible.
That was a rather secular home.
Even the school that we went to, that was near where we lived and that was a Polish school-- I mean, Catholic school.
But, of course, we didn't take any religious subject.
We were studying Jewish religion on the side.
But we did observe the high holy days, and we knew that we were Jewish.
We were aware of it.
There was even some Zionistic in early childhood.
I remember-- that was in probably 1922 or '23-- my grandfather, maternal grandfather, with his wife and three youngest daughters, they liquidated everything in Poland and they went to Palestine.
Started to establish life there.
And it was very traumatic for my mother and for us.
I remember taking my grandfather with his family to the train station and saying goodbye.
But that was his idea, the Zionist, early Zionism.
Unfortunately, it didn't work out.
And after a year he came back losing all the hope and also the money that he planned to invest there.
Yes, that's what I wanted to mention.
I remember I had a lot being in school with Catholic girls, with Polish girls.
That was a girl's school.
I had many friends.
Some were even very close.
I trusted them to the point when once I was in their house and her older sister came from the university wearing the insignia of the anti-Semitic party.
And that made me almost sick.
Of course, I didn't tell anything at home because that was what my family expected.
But I know I was crying the whole night.
That was probably one of the first disappointments.
And, still, that was my dilemma, to continue friendship with this girl which was very sweet and good friend and taking position, which, as a child, it was very difficult for me.
So that was the first dilemma.
I had much more experiences later when I was invited to a party to my Christian friends, and sometimes I was the only one Jewish girl, sometimes another one.
And there were people from a different school.
They didn't know.
I didn't look Jewish.
And after a while, after a short time, started a conversation.
And, of course, the subject was very unpleasant to me, that was sarcastic towards Jews.
And I didn't know what to do, if I should leave or put my position or just ignore it.
So I was avoiding this as I grew up.

They were only unless I was very sure that I can rely, I knew the principles and ideas of my friend, I wouldn't expose myself [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to this anti-Semitism being alone, being a minority in a group of friends.
So as we were practically forced, a group of my friends were mostly Jewish girls.
It was growing-- graduate.
Of course, I started talking about the childhood, that naive children don't know anything.
Later, disappointed girl that her 10-year-old girlfriend is not as what I expected her to be.
But that was also rather childish.
But as we grew up, and we were coming closer to all this strength, it was more and more obvious.
And that was-- you know, when it really started to increase, that was because the trends were coming from Germany, which this part was greatly embraced in my own country.
And that was probably '35, '36 that the anti-Semitism was more obvious.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some of the Jewish businesses, they were boycott.
So, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, they wouldn't let the shoppers go to the store because that was a Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], Jewish business.
There were separation in schools.
That was, at first, put very mildly, equal but separate.
On universities, in colleges, there were Jewish students-- some of the colleges-- were permitted only to sit on the left side of the room and towards the end.
So that was definite separation.
That was economical, intellectual, social.
But, still, it was a pretty strong Jewish community, quite independent, so that was-- I would even say, it was a pleasant life maybe in the view of a young girl, 15, 16, 17-year-old.
Did your parents have any plans to go  into hiding or to leave the country?
No, no, no.
No, no, no.
I know there was no place to go within the family.
In other countries, we were not financially [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] extremely wealthy.
So that was never a plan of my family.
So that was the atmosphere in the country prior to the war.
I graduated high school.
I had one year of college, which was professional because that was already the practical trend.
So that was a study for hotel management [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] education.
And so I had only one year of it.
At that time, I met my future husband, and probably we knew that we will end up together.
So I was quite comfortable with whatever I did.
And so that was the personal life.
My sister was in first year of college, and also she felt that very strongly, this anti-Semitism trend.
But, otherwise, it was a nice social life, young students.
We had open house, always pleasant.
We had a lot of company, and we were not too serious about it.
We were getting already the news from Czechoslovakia, from Sudeten, from other parts, and we knew that if Hitler's policy will go into this direction, Poland will be the next country.
But there was nothing that we could do it.
Absolutely.
It was very, very passive.
We didn't belong to any organization specially, so we just heard information from the radio and from the press.
Well--
Yes.
So Poland was the first country to be invaded by Germany.
I was, at that time, in this city where the first bomb.
That was Krakow in Galatea.
And that was September 1st, 1939.
And, of course, there was a panic.
It was unexpected.
It wasn't declared war.
It was like a blitz, I think.
And of course, they were very uneven forces, so there was no more chance as far as war preparation And war machine.
And Poland, the whole country, fell into German hands within three weeks.
We were on the way.
That was in Krakow.
But when the war started we try to join-- of course, we didn't know what was in stock, what would happen.
But we wanted to be, we thought, further from the battlefront.
That's what we thought.
And we were heading toward Warsaw where the rest of our family-- we had a pretty large family in Warsaw.
And we wanted to be together.
So that was the best thing because practically we were facing the armies or the battles all the way along.
And we were part of every new conquered town on the way to Warsaw.
But after the Warsaw fell, that was the end of it.
And we joined our family in Warsaw.
That was October sometime, beginning of October '39. 39, yes.
And what happened then?
Of course, we lost everything.
That was immediately reprocessed by the German, by the, probably, local people.
So we had given up.
I mean, we didn't even try to recover anything.
But we stayed with the family in Warsaw.
But the tempo, the speed of the event, was so quick that you couldn't even think about it.
There were immediately new rules and decrees and regulations and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] done in a very strict way.
And end of every regulation was if you would not comply, you would be shot to death.
So there were no choices, and everything had be done very speedily, like moving from one location to another.
Let's say, when you have a better apartment, better house, in a better section, that was immediately evacuated for the German officials.
Then you went down the line.
And eventually, we all ended up in the ghetto which was already [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 1940.
The ghetto was more or less established, the borders of the ghetto.
So that was, of course, the oldest and the poorest part of Warsaw.
And all the Polish occupants, they were offered other quarters in other parts of the city.
And all the Jewish population was supposed to get ahold of some living quarters within the borders of the ghetto.
Now let me go back before the ghetto, because that was important time for me.
I got married.
My husband was following me.
My husband was following me to Warsaw.
He escaped with his father, trying to save him [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the east part of Poland where they were from.
But he still decided to look for me.
And he came to Warsaw and we decided to get married at that time.

But one incident if, I might say, private, the personal, so as long as we still lived in our original homes, the German soldiers and all their supporters, either if they were Ukrainians or some other collaborators were permitted to come to the house to plunder, to rape.
They had full power over us.
So this group of younger, German, inexperienced soldiers came to the house, and they ask for jewelry or some other valuables.
We said that we already delivered accordingly to these [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] laws and decrees, but they still wanted this.
And they wanted me and another person in this house to undress so they could examine the clothes.
And then my future husband protested.
He embarrassed them in a way.
If I were German, [GERMAN], pure race, how they dare to expect a young woman to undress, you know?
And they beat him up and split.
So I was safe, and my wedding, my engagement ring, which was very important later.
So, anyway, that was the incident.
But I realized at that time that I have a luxury of having somebody that is protecting me.
At the same time, it's a big optimist, which was very helpful over those years.
He was taking care of me and my sister, Sophie Perutz.
I don't know if I mentioned, I have one sister.
She's two years older than I am.
And we went through the war together.
We were inseparable, and we survived together.
So all three of us [INAUDIBLE] so that's probably that concern of the three of us.
We did move into the ghetto together.
However, my sister, Sophie, lived with my family, with my parents.
And our first quarter, we got a little separate room, my husband and me.
And, of course, we started somehow to organize, to create a pleasant.
That's what we called home, house, whatever.
The attitude was we-- it was very hard to take care to find room for ourselves.
And here, we were squeezed together.
It was tighter and tighter.
But there was no way-- I cannot recall special incidents, special fights.
We were not happy about it, but we were also not happy when our quarters were shrinking and we had to accommodate more people to live together.
So I don't remember at this time in the beginning.
I would say that we cooperated and trying to be accommodating.
Was there animosity?
No, we had a very strong mutual enemy.
We had the enemy.
Survival was on the mind of everybody.
And probably there were some, and mostly this is later on, when we were deprived of humanity, of all the decent feelings.
They were, maybe, different acts.
And they are-- of course, the mean instincts of people that, to some extent, collaborated doing their job about maybe being more efficient than was expected from them.
So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in the ghetto, that was a sort of a Jewish council which was supposed to oversee the affairs of the ghetto and also maybe coordinate.
That's what we thought in the beginning.
The affair of the ghetto with the German government.
That was also establish the Jewish police force.
It was called 13th. 'Trzynasty.' That was in Polish.
And I am now-- and all those years, I was so happy that Henry-- my husband's name-- never joined them.
You know, at first I thought that maybe that would be something for him to do, but he said never.
He wouldn't put any sort of either uniform or take any orders.
And I am so grateful for it.
Even that, you know, some of these policeman did a very good job being helpful, but not all of them.
That was some.
It was a blame of this organization and some of the people.
So the life in the ghetto, besides this that we tried at first to organize that the borders of the ghetto where constantly changing.
So once you settled down, you thought you were fairly comfortable.
Except now, you have to move to another quarter because for this and this strategic reason, we have to have it.
So, again, run and look for something and carry whatever you were able to carry and settle down again and start from the beginning.
At first, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I mean, they were not-- working [INAUDIBLE], the slave labor.
That came a little bit later.
So at first, I don't recall this.
I don't remember.
They were people-- rather, I remember the selections and transports of people.
But the ones that were destined to work, the work wasn't immediate.
It wasn't waiting.
So that was in the process of organization.
And also, in the ghetto, in the very beginning, they tried to organize sort of schools, if that was possible, or orphanages or some workshop, some different kinds of studies.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's, you know, hard to imagine mentioning about it because the life, itself, in the ghetto was misery.
It's hunger, epidemic of typhoid fever, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people dying on the streets, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] complete deterioration of life from day to day.
So it's hard to imagine that still there were a lot of efforts which were, unfortunately, useless.
